FINANCIAL MODELLING RAJAKRISHNAN MANIVEL 2026-04-25 FINANCIAL MODELLING STEP-BY-STEP INSTRUCTION MANUAL This manual helps in learning basic Excel functions and 20 Comprehensive Exercises | Financial Analysis | VBA | Charts Contributors Dr. V. Mallika Associate Professor and Dean Dr. M. Rajakrishnan Assistant Professor Dr. R. Karthikeyan Assistant Professor Bridge Course on Excel Fundamentals  Download Excel File Download and Practice Excel File 0.1 Introduction to Excel What is Microsoft Excel Microsoft Excel is a spreadsheet software used for data entry, calculation, analysis, and visualization. Interface Overview Workbook: Excel file Worksheet: Sheet inside workbook Ribbon: Toolbar with commands Rows, Columns, Cells Rows: Horizontal (1,2,3…) Columns: Vertical (A,B,C…) Cell: Intersection (A1) Summary Excel organizes data in tabular format. Practice Exercises Identify cell address of 3rd row and 2nd column Create a simple table 0.2 Basic Formula Concepts Formula Syntax All formulas start with = Operators Addition Subtraction Multiplication / Division ^ Power Cell References Relative: A1 Absolute: $A$1 Mixed: A$1 or $A1 Example =A1+B1 Tip Use $ to lock cells in formulas. Summary Formulas always begin with = Practice Exercises Add two numbers using formula Try absolute reference 0.3 Mathematical Functions SUM() Syntax =SUM(A1:A5) Example A 10 20 30 Result: 60 AVERAGE() =AVERAGE(A1:A5) MIN() and MAX() =MIN(A1:A5) =MAX(A1:A5) COUNT() and COUNTA() =COUNT(A1:A5) =COUNTA(A1:A5) Common Mistakes Including text in numeric functions Summary Mathematical functions simplify calculations Practice Exercises Find sum of 5 numbers Find average marks 0.4 Logical Functions IF() Syntax =IF(A1>50,“Pass”,“Fail”) Example Marks 60 Result: Pass AND(), OR(), NOT() =AND(A1>50,B1>50) =OR(A1>50,B1>50) =NOT(A1>50) Tip Use nested IF for multiple conditions Summary Logical functions help in decision-making 0.5 Text Functions LEFT(), RIGHT(), MID() =LEFT(A1,3) =RIGHT(A1,2) =MID(A1,2,3) LEN() =LEN(A1) TRIM() =TRIM(A1) CONCAT() =CONCAT(A1,B1) Example First Last John Doe Result: JohnDoe Summary Text functions manipulate strings 0.6 Date & Time Functions TODAY() and NOW() =TODAY() =NOW() DATE() =DATE(2026,1,1) YEAR(), MONTH(), DAY() =YEAR(A1) =MONTH(A1) =DAY(A1) Summary Useful for reports and tracking 0.7 Lookup & Reference Functions VLOOKUP() =VLOOKUP(A2,A1:B10,2,FALSE) HLOOKUP() =HLOOKUP(A2,A1:D5,2,FALSE) XLOOKUP() =XLOOKUP(A2,A1:A10,B1:B10) INDEX + MATCH() =INDEX(B1:B10,MATCH(A2,A1:A10,0)) Warning VLOOKUP only works left to right Summary - Lookup functions retrieve data 0.8 Conditional Functions COUNTIF() =COUNTIF(A1:A10,“>50”) SUMIF() =SUMIF(A1:A10,“>50”) COUNTIFS() and SUMIFS() =COUNTIFS(A1:A10,“>50”,B1:B10,“Pass”) Summary - Useful for filtered calculations 0.9 Error Handling IFERROR() =IFERROR(A1/B1,“Error”) Common Errors - #DIV/0! - #N/A - #VALUE! Tip Always handle division errors 0.10 Practical Applications Salary Sheet Example Name Salary Bonus Total A 10000 2000 =B2+C2 Student Marks =IF(A2>=50,“Pass”,“Fail”) Business Report =SUM(B2:B10) Final Summary - Excel formulas automate calculations - Practice is key to mastery Final Practice Create student result sheet Build sales report Apply IF and SUMIF together Conclusion You now understand the fundamental Excel formulas and how to apply them in real-world scenarios.

Exercise 1 Sales Data Analysis (2015–2020) Learning Outcomes Analyse multi-region sales data Apply SUM, AVERAGE, and Growth formulas Create charts for comparison and trends Perform sorting and filtering 1.1 Dataset Region Year Sales North 2015 42000 North 2016 46800 North 2017 51200 North 2018 55600 North 2019 60100 North 2020 65400 South 2015 35000 South 2016 38500 South 2017 41200 South 2018 44800 South 2019 48500 South 2020 53200 East 2015 28000 East 2016 31200 East 2017 35600 East 2018 39400 East 2019 43800 East 2020 49100 West 2015 32000 West 2016 35800 West 2017 40100 West 2018 44200 West 2019 49600 West 2020 55300  Download Excel File Download Sales Data Excel 1.2 Data Entry & Formulas 1.2.1 Structure in Excel Column Description A Region B–G Sales (2015–2020) H Total I Average J Growth % 1.2.2 Formulas Used Calculation Formula Total =SUM(B4:G4) Average =AVERAGE(B4:G4) Growth % =(G4-B4)/B4 1.2.3 Grand Total Row Calculation Formula Year-wise Total =SUM(B4:B7) Overall Total =SUM(H4:H7) Avg of Avg =AVERAGE(I4:I7) 1.3 Chart Creation 1.3.1 Recommended Charts Line Chart → Trend over years Column Chart → Region comparison 1.3.2 Diagram flowchart TD A[Select Data] --> B[Insert Tab] B --> C[Choose Chart] 1.3.3 Insight North region has highest sales All regions show upward growth trend 1.4 Chart Formatting Title: Regional Sales Trend (2015–2020) X-axis: Year Y-axis: Sales Legend: Regions 1.5 Interpretation Sales across all regions show steady growth. North region leads consistently, while East shows gradual improvement. Overall performance indicates positive business expansion. 1.6 Sorting 1.6.1 Example Sort by 2020 Sales → Identify top region Sort by Growth % → Identify fastest growing region 1.7 Advanced Filter 1.7.1 Example Criteria Region North OR Growth % >0.5 1.8 Final Output Multi-region Excel analysis Charts showing trends and comparison Summary interpretation 1.9 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates how Excel tools can be used effectively for business data analysis, visualization, and decision-making.

Exercise 2 Financial Data Analysis & Reporting Learning Outcomes Analyse company financial performance Apply ranking, logical, and statistical formulas Perform filtering and risk analysis Interpret financial indicators for decision-making 2.1 Dataset Company Revenue (Cr) Profit (Cr) EPS (Rs) ROE (%) Debt/Equity Reliance Industries 845000 75000 110 18 0.8 Tata Steel 150500 12800 42 14 1.2 HDFC Bank 160000 35000 60 20 0.5 Infosys 150000 32000 55 23 0.3 ITC 58500 15200 39 18 0.4  Download Excel File Download Financial Data Excel 2.2 Data Entry & Formulas 2.2.1 Additional Columns in Excel Column Purpose Formula Rank by Profit Profit ranking =RANK(C4,C$4:C$8,0) Rank by ROE ROE ranking =RANK(E4,E$4:E$8,0) Risk Level Categorisation =IF(F4>1,“High Risk”,IF(F4>0.6,“Medium”,“Low Risk”)) Filter Result Condition check =IF(AND(E4>0.15,F4<1),“Qualifies”,“Excluded”) 2.3 Chart Creation 2.3.1 Recommended Charts Column Chart → Compare Profit & Revenue Bar Chart → Compare ROE across companies flowchart TD A[Select Data] --> B[Insert Tab] B --> C[Choose Chart] 2.3.2 Insight Reliance has highest revenue and profit Infosys has highest ROE 2.4 Chart Formatting Title: Company Financial Comparison X-axis: Company Y-axis: Values Legend: Metrics 2.5 Interpretation Reliance Industries dominates in revenue and profit. Infosys shows strong efficiency with highest ROE. Tata Steel has higher financial risk due to high debt-equity ratio. 2.6 Sorting 2.6.1 Example Sort by Profit → Identify top company Sort by ROE → Identify best performing company 2.7 : Advanced Filter 2.7.1 Criteria ROE (%) Debt/Equity >15 <1 2.7.2 Output Reliance Industries HDFC Bank Infosys ITC 2.8 Conditional Formatting Highlight Debt/Equity > 1 → Tata Steel (High Risk) 2.9 Summary Statistics Metric Formula Highest Revenue =MAX(B4:B8) Highest Profit =MAX(C4:C8) Best EPS =MAX(D4:D8) Avg ROE =AVERAGE(E4:E8) Lowest D/E =MIN(F4:F8) Companies ROE>15% =COUNTIF(E4:E8,“>0.15”) 2.10 Final Output Financial dataset with rankings Risk classification Filtered company list Summary insights 2.11 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates advanced Excel techniques for financial analysis including ranking, logical evaluation, and performance comparison.

Exercise 3 Share, Bond & Debenture Valuation (PRICE & YIELD Functions) 3.1 Learning Outcomes Analyse automobile company share data Apply financial formulas (Gordon Growth Model, P/E Ratio, Dividend Yield) Create charts for valuation comparison Perform sorting and filtering for investment analysis 3.2 Dataset Company EPS (Rs) DIV % Share Price (Rs) Face Value (Rs) Annual Dividend (Rs) Dividend Yield % P/E Ratio Intrinsic Value* Over/Under Valued Bajaj 11.9 0.50 275.0 10 5.0 1.82% 23.11 71.43 Overvalued Hero Honda 10.2 0.22 135.0 10 2.2 1.63% 13.24 31.43 Overvalued Kinetic 12.0 0.25 177.5 10 2.5 1.41% 14.79 35.71 Overvalued M & M 20.1 0.25 205.0 10 2.5 1.22% 10.20 35.71 Overvalued * Intrinsic Value assumes Required Return (K)=12%, Growth Rate (g)=5%  Download Excel File Download Financial Data Excel 3.3 Data Entry & Formulas 3.3.1 Structure in Excel Column Description A Company B–E Inputs (EPS, DIV%, Price, Face Value) F Annual Dividend G Dividend Yield % H P/E Ratio I Intrinsic Value* J Over/Under Valued 3.3.2 Formulas Used (Assuming data starts in Row 3) Calculation Formula Annual Dividend =E3*C3 (Face Value × DIV %) Dividend Yield % =F3/D3 (Annual Dividend / Share Price) P/E Ratio =D3/B3 (Share Price / EPS) Intrinsic Value =F3/(0.12-0.05) (Annual Div / (K - g)) Over/Under Valued =IF(D3>I3, "Overvalued", "Undervalued") 3.3.3 Averages Row (Bottom Row) Calculation Formula Average EPS =AVERAGE(B3:B6) Average P/E Ratio =AVERAGE(H3:H6) Average Yield =AVERAGE(G3:G6) 3.4 Chart Creation 3.4.1 Recommended Charts Clustered Column Chart → Compare Share Price vs. Intrinsic Value Bar Chart → P/E Ratio comparison across companies 3.4.2 Diagram flowchart TD A[Select Data] --> B[Insert Tab] B --> C[Choose Chart] 3.4.3 Insight All analyzed automobile shares are currently trading above their calculated intrinsic value. M & M offers the lowest P/E ratio, while Bajaj has the highest dividend yield. 3.5 Chart Formatting Title: Share Price vs. Intrinsic Value X-axis: Company Y-axis: Value (Rs) Legend: Share Price, Intrinsic Value 3.6 Interpretation The analysis reveals that the shares across all four automobile companies (Bajaj, Hero Honda, Kinetic, and M & M) are currently overvalued based on the Gordon Growth Model (assuming a 12% required return and 5% growth rate). Despite being overvalued, Bajaj offers the best dividend yield at 1.82%, while M & M appears relatively cheaper compared to earnings with the lowest P/E ratio of 10.20. 3.7 Sorting 3.7.1 Example Sort by P/E Ratio (Smallest to Largest) → Identify the cheapest stock relative to earnings. Sort by Dividend Yield % (Largest to Smallest) → Identify the best income-generating stock. 3.8 Advanced Filter 3.8.1 Example Criteria Criteria 1: | Over/Under Valued | |——–| | Undervalued | OR Criteria 2: | P/E Ratio | |———-| | < 15 | 3.9 Final Output Multi-company financial Excel analysis Charts showing valuation comparisons Summary interpretation of stock pricing 3.10 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates how Excel tools and financial formulas can be used effectively for stock valuation, comparative analysis, and investment decision-making.

Exercise 4 IF Function: D/E Ratio Risk Analysis + COUNT Functions Learning Outcomes Analyse company debt-equity data for risk assessment Apply logical formulas (IF, Nested IF) for risk classification Use statistical functions (COUNT, COUNTA, COUNTIF, COUNTIFS) Create charts for leverage comparison 4.1 Dataset Company Debt (Rs) Equity (Rs) D/E Ratio Risk Level Leverage Category Interest Coverage Investment Recommendation Company A 500 1000 0.5 Low Under-Leveraged 4.00 BUY Company B 1200 800 1.5 High Over-Leveraged 1.33 CAUTION Company C 300 1500 0.2 Low Under-Leveraged 10.00 BUY Company D 700 1000 0.7 Medium Under-Leveraged 2.86 BUY D/E Risk Classification: - < 0.5 → Low - 0.5 – 1.0 → Medium - 1.0 – 1.5 → High - > 1.5 → Very High 4.2  Download Excel File Download Debt-Equity Data Excel 4.3 Data Entry & Formulas 4.3.1 Structure in Excel Column Description A Company B–C Inputs (Debt, Equity) D D/E Ratio E Risk Level F Leverage Category G Interest Coverage H Investment Recommendation 4.3.2 Formulas Used Calculation Formula D/E Ratio =B3/C3 Risk Level =IF(D3<0.5,“Low”,IF(D3<=1,“Medium”,IF(D3<=1.5,“High”,“Very High”))) Leverage Category =IF(D3>1,“Over-Leveraged”,“Under-Leveraged”) 4.4 COUNT Functions Analysis Calculation Formula Count numeric D/E values =COUNT(D3:D6) Count non-blank companies =COUNTA(A3:A6) Companies with D/E > 1 =COUNTIF(D3:D6,“>1”) High Risk companies =COUNTIF(E3:E6,“High”) Empty cells =COUNTBLANK(D3:D6) 4.5 Chart Creation 4.5.1 Recommended Charts Clustered Column Chart → Compare Debt vs Equity Pie Chart → Risk distribution 4.5.2 Diagram flowchart TD A[Select Data] --> B[Insert Tab] B --> C[Choose Chart] 4.5.3 Insight Company B is over-leveraged (high risk) Majority companies are financially stable 4.6 Chart Formatting Title: Company Debt vs Equity Comparison X-axis: Company Y-axis: Amount (Rs) Legend: Debt, Equity 4.7 Interpretation Companies A, C, and D maintain healthy financial structures with lower debt levels, making them suitable for investment. Company B shows higher financial risk due to excessive leverage and lower interest coverage. 4.8 Sorting Example Sort by D/E Ratio (Descending) -> Identify highest risk Sort by Interest Coverage (Ascending) -> Identify weakest firms 4.9 Advanced Filter Criteria Risk Level Low OR Investment Recommendation BUY 4.10 Final Output Risk classification using IF formulas Statistical summary using COUNT functions Chart-based comparison Investment decision insights 4.11 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates how Excel logical and statistical functions can be used for financial risk evaluation, enabling automated and data-driven decision-making.

Exercise 5 NPV Profile: Net Present Value at Different Discount Rates 5.1 What is NPV? 5.2 Dataset 5.3 Data Entry & Formulas 5.4 Chart Creation 5.5 Chart Formatting 5.6 Interpretation 5.7 Sorting 5.8 Advanced Filter 5.9 Final Output 5.10 Conclusion

Exercise 5 NPV Profile: Net Present Value at Different Discount Rates Learning Outcomes Analyse project cash flows to determine Net Present Value (NPV) Apply financial formulas (NPV) at varying discount rates Determine the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) conceptually using an NPV Profile Create an NPV Profile chart to visualize project viability and sensitivity 5.1 What is NPV? NPV stands for Net Present Value. It tells you the current value of future cash flows, after subtracting the initial investment. It is used to evaluate investment projects or business decisions. Core Concept: It calculates how much future money is worth today, considering time and risk. Purpose: NPV helps compare today’s cost with today’s value of future income. 5.1.1 Interpretation NPV > 0 → Accept the project (it’s profitable) NPV < 0 → Reject the project (it’s a loss) NPV = 0 → Break-even 5.1.2 Example Imagine someone promises to give you ₹1,000 one year from now. Would you value it as ₹1,000 today? No — because: ₹1,000 today can be invested and grow over time. There’s a risk you may not receive it. Due to inflation, ₹1,000 in the future buys less than it does today. So, the present value (today’s value) of that ₹1,000 is less than ₹1,000 — maybe around ₹909 if the interest rate is 10%. ₹909.09 today is equal in value to ₹1,000 one year from now at a 10% discount rate. 5.2 Dataset 5.2.1 Cash Flow Data Year Cash Flow (Rs) Description 0 -20000 Initial Investment 1 8000 Year 1 Inflow 2 7000 Year 2 Inflow 3 6000 Year 3 Inflow 4 5000 Year 4 Inflow 5 4400 Year 5 Inflow 6 2400 Year 6 Inflow 5.2.2 NPV Profile Table Discount Rate NPV (Rs) Status Interpretation 0% 12800.00 Accept Project adds Value 5% 8503.22 Accept Project adds Value 10% 5067.60 Accept Project adds Value 15% 2278.55 Accept Project adds Value 16% 1784.05 Accept Project adds Value 20% -16.72 Reject Project destroys value 25% -1929.06 Reject Project destroys value * IRR ≈ the discount rate where NPV = 0. From the table, IRR falls between 16% and 20%.  Download Excel File Download Financial Data Excel 5.3 Data Entry & Formulas 5.3.1 Structure in Excel Column Description A Discount Rate B NPV (Rs) C Status D Interpretation 5.3.2 Formulas Used Calculation Formula NPV =NPV(rate, B5:B10) + B4 (Add initial investment separately as it occurs at Year 0) Status =IF(B14>0, "Accept", "Reject") Interpretation =IF(C14="Accept", "Project adds Value", "Project destroys value") 5.4 Chart Creation 5.4.1 Recommended Charts Line Chart (NPV Profile) -> Plot Discount Rate on X-axis and NPV on Y-axis to visualize where the line crosses zero (the IRR). 5.4.2 Diagram flowchart TD A[Calculate NPVs] --> B[Insert Line Chart] B --> C[Identify IRR at X-Axis intercept] 5.4.3 Insight The project adds value at discount rates up to approximately 19.9%. The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is between 16% and 20% (since NPV turns negative at 20%). 5.5 Chart Formatting Title: NPV Profile: Sensitivity to Discount Rates X-axis: Discount Rate (%) Y-axis: Net Present Value (Rs) Legend: NPV 5.6 Interpretation The NPV profile illustrates the inverse relationship between the discount rate and the project’s Net Present Value. As the required rate of return (cost of capital) increases, the NPV steadily declines. The project is financially viable (NPV > 0) for discount rates up to roughly 19.9%. At a 20% discount rate, the NPV turns negative (-16.72 Rs), indicating that the project’s exact Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is just under 20%. 5.7 Sorting 5.7.1 Example Sort by NPV (Descending) -> Observe the highest potential value creation at lower discount rates. 5.8 Advanced Filter 5.8.1 Example Criteria Criteria 1: Status Accept OR Criteria 2: NPV (Rs) > 5000 5.9 Final Output Cash flow tabulation and NPV computation across multiple rates Logical IF formulation for investment Accept/Reject status NPV Profile Line Chart visualizing the IRR intercept Capital budgeting decision insights 5.10 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates how to construct an NPV Profile using Excel’s financial functions. By evaluating NPV across various discount rates, decision-makers can determine the project’s IRR visually and assess its sensitivity to changes in the cost of capital.

Exercise 6 NPV & IRR: Project X vs Project Y (Mutually Exclusive)” 6.1 Dataset 6.2 Data Entry & Formulas 6.3 Chart Creation 6.4 Chart Formatting 6.5 Interpretation 6.6 Sorting 6.7 Advanced Filter 6.8 Final Output 6.9 Conclusion

Exercise 6 NPV & IRR: Project X vs Project Y (Mutually Exclusive)” Learning Outcomes Compute after-tax cash flows (CFAT) from cash flow before tax (CFBT) Apply financial functions (NPV and IRR) to evaluate project feasibility Compare mutually exclusive projects to make investment decisions Create charts for cash flow trends and valuation comparison 6.1 Dataset  Download Excel File Download Financial Data Excel 6.1.1 Project Assumptions Cost of Capital (K): 10% Tax Rate: 35% Parameter Project X Project Y Project Cost (Rs) 20,000 15,000 Life (Years) 5 5 Annual Depreciation (Rs) 4,000 3,000 Salvage Value (Rs) 0 0 6.1.2 Cash Flow Calculations Year CFBT Dep EBT Tax (35%) EAT CFAT Project X 1-5 10,000 4,000 6,000 2,100 3,900 7,900 Project Y 1-5 8,000 3,000 5,000 1,750 3,250 6,250 6.1.3 NPV & IRR Results Metric Project X Project Y Status Initial Investment -20,000 -15,000 - NPV (Rs) 9,947.22 8,692.42 Accept IRR (%) 28.01% 30.77% Accept * Decision Rule: Both projects have an IRR > Cost of Capital (10%), making them acceptable. Since they are mutually exclusive, select the project with the higher NPV. 6.2 Data Entry & Formulas 6.2.1 Structure in Excel Column Description A Year B-G Project X Cash Flow Breakdown (CFBT to CFAT) H-L Project Y Cash Flow Breakdown (CFBT to CFAT) Bottom Rows NPV and IRR Calculations 6.2.2 Formulas Used Calculation Formula Depreciation =(Cost - Salvage) / Life EBT =CFBT - Depreciation Tax =EBT * 0.35 CFAT =EBT - Tax + Depreciation (or =EAT + Depreciation) NPV =NPV(0.10, CFAT_Range) - Initial_Cost (Note: Initial cost subtracted since it’s an outflow) IRR =IRR(CF_Range) (Include Year 0 initial cost as a negative in the range) 6.3 Chart Creation 6.3.1 Recommended Charts Clustered Column Chart -> Compare the NPV and Initial Cost between Project X and Project Y Line Chart -> Compare cumulative CFAT across the 5 years for both projects 6.3.2 Diagram flowchart TD A[Calculate CFAT for X & Y] --> B[Calculate NPV & IRR] B --> C{Are projects Mutually Exclusive?} C -->|Yes| D[Select Project with Higher NPV] 6.3.3 Insight Project X generates a higher absolute value (NPV of Rs. 9,947.22). Project Y generates a higher percentage return (IRR of 30.77%), requiring less initial capital. 6.4 Chart Formatting Title: Project Valuation Comparison: NPV vs IRR X-axis: Project (X vs Y) Y-axis (Primary): Net Present Value (Rs) Y-axis (Secondary): Internal Rate of Return (%) Legend: NPV, IRR 6.5 Interpretation Both projects are financially viable as their Internal Rates of Return (28.01% and 30.77%) exceed the required cost of capital (10%), and both yield positive Net Present Values. However, because these are mutually exclusive projects (only one can be chosen), the decision rule dictates selecting the project with the highest NPV. Therefore, Project X should be accepted as it creates more total wealth (Rs 9,947.22) compared to Project Y (Rs 8,692.42), despite Project Y having a slightly higher percentage return. If capital rationing is a factor (limited funds), a Profitability Index (PI) analysis would be recommended. 6.6 Sorting 6.6.1 Example Sort by CFAT (Descending) -> Identify which project generates higher annual liquidity. 6.7 Advanced Filter 6.7.1 Example Criteria Criteria 1: IRR > 10% (Cost of Capital) AND Criteria 2: NPV (Rs) > 0 6.8 Final Output Tax and depreciation adjustments computed dynamically NPV and IRR outputs calculated for multiple project pipelines Capital budgeting decisions formulated based on mutually exclusive conditions Charts highlighting risk-return differences 6.9 Conclusion This experiment demonstrates how to structure complex capital budgeting decisions in Excel. By comparing Mutually Exclusive projects, we learn that while IRR highlights capital efficiency, NPV must be the deciding factor as it represents the absolute magnitude of wealth generated.
